The dwarf spheroidal galaxies without obvious sings of star formation are located inside the R_0 zones, demonstrating the well-known segregation effect of the early type galaxies ( T < 0 ) against the late type ones in dependence on the density of environment. From the data of Table table3 follows that the average weighted ratio of the total mass-to- K band luminosity for the M 63, M 51, and M 101 groups is (12 4) M_ /L_ . According to Driver2012 , the average global density of the K- luminosity is equal to (4.3 0.2) 10^8 L_ /Mpc ^3 at H_0=73 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 . At the value of the critical cosmic density 1.46 10^ 11 M_ Mpc ^ 3 and the parameter _m = 0.3 , the ratio of the global density of dark matter to the global density of K luminosity is (102 5) M_ /L_ . Therefore, the ratio of dark-to-luminous matter in the M 63/ M 51/ M 101 filament groups turns out to be 8.5 times less than the global cosmic ratio.

sources.summary: The paper presents SWAN NOEMA+30m maps of M 51, describing it as a nearby face-on spiral with a low-luminosity AGN and finding cloud-scale dense-gas tracer variations across its disk and center.

quotes.quote: We present the first results from "Surveying the Whirlpool at Arcseconds with NOEMA" (SWAN), an IRAM Northern Extended Millimetre Array (NOEMA)+30m large program that maps emission from several molecular lines at 90 and 110 GHz in the iconic nearby grand-design spiral galaxy M 51 at cloud-scale resolution ( 3 =125 pc). As part of this work, we have obtained the first sensitive cloud-scale map of of the inner 5 7 kpc of a normal star-forming galaxy, which we compare to and emission to test their ability in tracing dense, star-forming gas. The average to- line ratio of our total FoV is 0.20 0.09 , with strong regional variations of a factor of 2 throughout the disk, including the south-western spiral arm and the center. The central 1 kpc exhibits elevated emission compared to , probably caused by AGN-driven excitation effects.

M 51 (NGC 5194) is a nearby D = 8.58 Mpc; mcquinn_distance_2016 close to face-on i = 22 ^ , P.A. = 173 ^ ; colombo_pdbi_2014 massive log _ 10 M _ / M _ = 10.5 ; den_brok_co_2022 spiral galaxy that hosts a low-luminosity AGN ho_search_1997, dumas_local_2011, querejeta_agn_2016 . has been mapped at 3 \ resolution (125 pc) for three circular regions of 3 kpc diameter querejeta_dense_2019 in M 51, at 4 in the outer spiral arm at 5 kpc galactocentric distance chen_dense_2017 and out to 8 kpc in the disk at 1--2 kpc resolution by EMPIRE bigiel_empire_2016, jimenez-donaire_empire_2019 . watanabe_spectral_2014 detected both and at kpc resolution in two 30m pointings in the south-western spiral arm, and den_brok_co_2022 presented observations of its center at kpc resolution.

sources.summary: The paper uses M 51 to study dust grain alignment, concluding that B-RAT dominates across the spiral galaxy while radiation-driven disruption changes dust sizes between arms and interarms.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we aim to characterize the dust grain alignment mechanisms across the spiral galaxy M 51. M 51 is the most studied face-on spiral galaxy using radio Fletcher2011,Kierdorf2020 and FIR Jones2020,Borlaff2021 polarimetric observations. These studies have shown that M 51 has an ordered spiral B-field morphology closely following the spiral arms of the galaxy in both radio and FIR polarization, tracing B-fields in the diffuse and dense ISM, respectively.

We have presented the first empirical study of the several dust grain alignment mechanisms in the ISM of the nearby spiral galaxy, M 51. Our analyses show that magnetically aligned dust grains (B-RAT) is the dominant dust alignment mechanism across the galaxy. We estimated that other alignment mechanisms, randomization (gas dumping), radiative precession (k-RAT), and mechanical alignment (v-MAT), are negligible in M 51. We estimated that the dust grain sizes change as a function of the galaxy's components.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HST-WFPC2 star clusters in interacting galaxy M51, deriving cluster properties and finding disruption and initial mass-function parameters largely independent of distance from its center.

quotes.quote: We present the results of an analysis of a population of stellar clusters in the interacting galaxy, M51, using HST-WFPC2 observations. The observations were made in five broad band filters; f336w (U), f439w (B), f555w (V), f675w (R), f814w (I), and transformed to the closest corresponding Cousins-Johnson filter magnitude using the equations defined by Holtzmann . Only sources that were found in every filter were analysed. By comparing the spectral energy distribution (SED) of each source with those those of evolutionary spectral synthesis models , we have derived an age, mass, and extinction for each source.

Following the work of Boutloukos Lamers (2002, BL02), we have searched our sample for evidence of cluster disruption. Our sample has the benefit over previous studies of disruption in M51 of covering a much larger area of the galaxy. The deficiency of this study is the brighter detection limit which hampers our analysis.

sources.summary: The paper compares M51 extinction measurements, finding high HII-region attenuation, limited radial-gradient evidence, and a secondary UV-extinction peak associated with its obscured companion.

quotes.quote: For a few galaxies, independent measures of the dust extinction in HII regions are available: scoville01 used high resolution and (Paschen- ) images of M51 to study the extinction in HII regions. For illustration, we add their results to the top row-middle column panel of Fig. figM51 . Each triangle corresponds to an individual HII region, in which the extinction has been computed from the to ratio. On average, they find a larger extinction than the one derived from the Balmer decrement at the same radius.

Only for M51 is a secondary peak observed, whose radius corresponds to its companion galaxy. Indeed the companion suffers very strong extinction since it is not visible in the UV image while being prominent in FIR. The UV and FIR surface brightness measured in the images at the position of the companion give a local extinction of 3 magnitudes.

sources.summary: The paper finds that M51 star clusters disrupt much faster than predicted, attributing the discrepancy to strong spiral-arm tidal-field variations, molecular clouds, and possibly a recent star-formation event.

quotes.quote: The disruption times of star clusters in M51 within 1 - 5 kpc from the nucleus, is shorter than predicted by about an order of magnitude. This discrepancy might be due to the strong tidal field variations in M51, caused by the strong density contrast between the spiral arms and interarm regions, or to the disruptive forces from giant molecular clouds.

M51 : The clusters are at galactocentric distances of 1 to 5 kpc. The column density of M51 decreases exponentially as =540 (-/4.65) , with in kpc (Salo Laurakainen 2000). The total disk mass within a ring of 1 to 5 kpc is 2.0 10^ 10 \ and the mean surface density in that annulus is 2.6 10^2 .

Since M51 experienced a recent star formation event, about 60 Myr ago (Bastian et al. 2004), possibly part of the discrepancy can be explained by variations in the IMF. M51 has the highest density contrast between the arm and interarm regions of any spiral galaxy (Rix Rieke 1993), so the tidal field variations experienced by star clusters will be higher than normal, which might explain the short disruption time of the star clusters in M51.

sources.summary: The chapter describes M51 as a galaxy with a complicated magnetic structure, including separate disc and halo fields, a disc reversal, significant asymmetry, and a dynamo model tied to its spiral structure.

quotes.quote: A striking example of a complicated magnetic structure that can hardly be explained by any mechanism other than the dynamo has been revealed in the galaxy M51 by Berkhuijsen \ (1997). These authors used radio polarization observations of the galaxy at wavelengths 2.8, 6.2, 18.0 and 20.5 (smoothed to a resolution of 3.5 ). The disc of this galaxy is not transparent to polarized radio emission at the two longer wavelengths. Therefore, it was possible to determine the magnetic field structure separately in two regions along the line the sight, which can be identified with the disc and halo of the galaxy.

As shown in Fig. M51 , the regular magnetic fields in the disc is reversed in a region about 3 by 8 kpc in size extended along azimuth at galactocentric radii s=3 -6 kpc and azimuthal angles 300^ -0 (shown with red arrows). A significant deviation from axial symmetry in the disc has been detected out to s=9 (in the azimuth range 160-- 260^ ), although it is too weak to result in a magnetic field reversal. The field reversal occurs around the corotation radius in M51, s 6 (i.e., the radius where the angular velocity of the spiral pattern is equal to that of the gas).

sources.summary: The paper measures M51's star-cluster luminosity function, finds a double-power-law break fainter than in the Antennae, and argues M51's high cluster formation rate reveals an upper cluster-mass limit.

quotes.quote: We present the luminosity function (LF) of star clusters in M51 based on HST/ACS observations taken as part of the Hubble Heritage project. The clusters are selected based on their size and with the resulting 5 990 clusters we present one of the largest cluster samples of a single galaxy. We find that the LF can be approximated with a double power-law distribution with a break around M_V = -8.9 .

The location of the bend, however, occurs about 1.6 mag fainter in M51. We confront the observed LF with the model for the evolution of integrated properties of cluster populations of gieles05b , which predicts that a truncated cluster initial mass function would result in a bend in, and a double power-law behaviour of, the integrated LF. The combination of the large field-of view and the high star cluster formation rate of M51 make it possible to detect such a bend in the LF. Hence, we conclude that there exists a fundamental upper limit to the mass of star clusters in M51.

sources.summary: The paper describes M51 (NGC 5194) as a nearby face-on Sc spiral with many young clusters, high star formation possibly driven by NGC 5195, and a cluster surface-density bump near corotation.

quotes.quote: The young ( <1 Gyr) star cluster population of M51 (NGC 5194, Hubble-type Sc) is, due to its relative proximity and high number of star clusters, an excellent candidate for studies to the formation and evolution of young star clusters (YCs) in spiral disks. M51 has many star clusters because of its high star formation rate, which may have been caused by its interaction with NGC 5195, and because the disk is almost face-on, we can observe the clusters throughout the disk without being hampered by extinction too much. The new HST/ACS mosaic image of M51, taken as part of the Hubble Heritage program , covers the entire disk in 6 pointings in F435W ( B ), F555W ( V ), F814W ( I ) and F658N ( H ), with a resolution of 2 pc per pixel. Previous studies on the star cluster population of M51 used lower resolution WFPC2 data that did not cover the entire disk (see e.g. ).

We use the sample of 4357 resolved clusters to plot the surface density distribution of the clusters in the disk of M51. Fig. 1.a. shows a bump in the surface density distribution at a galactocentric distance of 6 kpc. This distance is remarkably similar to the corotation radius of 5.5 1.0 kpc, which is the distance where the rotational velocity of the stellar and gaseous component is the same as the one of the spiral arms .

sources.summary: The paper maps M51 in CO, describing its face-on interacting spiral structure, molecular and atomic gas distribution, star-formation rate, Toomre stability, and molecular cloud properties.

quotes.quote: The nearby, almost face-on, and interacting galaxy M51 offers an excellent opportunity to study the distribution of molecular gas and the mechanisms governing the star formation rate. We have created a complete map (Fig. fig_m51 ) of M51 in ^ 12 CO 2--1 at a resolution of 11'' corresponding to 450 kpc using HERA at the IRAM-30m telescope. In we have combined these data with maps of HI and the radio-continuum to study the star formation efficiency, the local Schmidt law, and Toomre stability of the disk in radial averages out to radii of 12 kpc.

M51 is an interacting, grand-design spiral galaxy at a distance of only 8.4 Mpc seen nearly face-on. The emission detected with the 30m telescope (Fig. fig_m51 ) traces the well known two-armed spiral pattern out to the companion galaxy NGC 5195, which shows up brightly in the north-east at 10.5 kpc radial distance, and out to the south-western tip of the second arm at the opposite side of M51.
The large-scale distribution of the 21 cm line of atomic hydrogen in M51 was analyzed by using the VLA. We derive a global star formation rate of 2.56 yr ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper presents M51 as a nearby interacting system suited to HST cluster work, with face-on structure, past encounters, young spiral-arm clusters, and older red clusters across the system.

quotes.quote: M51 is located at a distance of 8.4 Mpc fel97 so that it is one of the most suitable interacting galaxies for star cluster survey using the HST . M51 is an interacting galaxy system that is composed of a face-on Sbc-type spiral galaxy NGC 5194 and a fainter companion, the SB0 galaxy NGC 5195. It is abundant with interstellar media (see a recent study by schu07 ).

The selected clusters are mostly found around the spiral arms of NGC 5194. Most of the star clusters are bluer than (B-V)=0.5 and (V-I)=0.8 , suggesting that they are young clusters with t 10^8 yrs. However, there are red clusters around both NGC 5194 and NGC 5195.

sources.summary: The paper models M51 as an interacting grand-design spiral whose present arm structure, kinks, bifurcations, and non-global pattern speed arise from interaction with NGC 5195.

quotes.quote: We present hydrodynamical models of the grand design spiral M51 (NGC 5194), and its interaction with its companion NGC 5195. Despite the simplicity of our models, our simulations capture the present day spiral structure of M51 remarkably well, and even reproduce details such as a kink along one spiral arm, and spiral arm bifurcations. We investigate the offset between the stellar and gaseous spiral arms, and find at most times (including the present day) there is no offset between the stars and gas to within our error bars.

We compute the pattern speed versus radius, and like the observations, find no single global pattern speed. We also show that the spiral arms cannot be fitted well by logarithmic spirals. We interpret these findings as evidence that M51 does not exhibit a quasi-steady density wave, as would be predicted by density wave theory.

sources.summary: The paper maps HII-region electron densities across M51, finding radius-density and galactocentric trends, plus an arm-crossing envelope peaking on spiral-arm ridge lines.

quotes.quote: Using ACS-HST images to yield continuum subtracted photometric maps in of the Sbc galaxy M51 and the dwarf irregular galaxy NGC 4449, we produced extensive (over 2000 regions for M51, over 200 regions for NGC4449) catalogues of parameters of their regions: their luminosities, equivalent radii and coordinates with respect to the galaxy centers. From these data we derived, for each region, its mean luminosity weighted electron density, n_e , determined from the luminosity and the radius, R , of the region.

Plotting these densities against the radii of the regions we find excellent fits for n_e varying as R^ 1/2 . This relatively simple relation has not, as far as we know, been predicted from models of region structure, and should be useful in constraining future models. Plotting the densities against the galactocentric radii, , of the regions we find good exponential fits, with scale lengths of close to 10 kpc for both galaxies.
We also plot the electron density of the regions across the spiral arms of M51, showing an envelope which peaks along the ridge lines of the arms.

sources.summary: The paper maps molecular gas in M 51 spiral arms and finds GMC properties and CO conversion factors broadly similar to Milky Way clouds despite strong spiral-arm star formation.

quotes.quote: Two selected regions in the molecular gas spiral arms in M 51 were mapped with the Owens Valley Radio Observatory (OVRO) mm-interferometer in the , , , HCN(1-0) and HCO+(1-0) emission lines. The CO data have been combined with the \ data from covering the central 3.5 kpc to study the physical properties of the molecular gas.

Our findings suggest that the GMC population in the spiral arms of M 51 is similar to those of the Milky Way and therefore the strong star formation occurring in the spiral arms has no strong impact on the molecular gas in the spiral arms.

The Whirlpool galaxy M 51 is an ideal target being one of the closest D=8.4 Mpc with 1'' 40.7 pc; fel97 almost face-on grand-design spiral galaxies. A significant fraction of its molecular gas is found in the spectacular spiral arms e.g. sco83,aal99,shet07,kod09 . Therefore it offers an unique opportunity to study the physical properties of the molecular gas within the spiral arms.

sources.summary: The paper uses an M 51-based tidal-interaction simulation, treating the main galaxy and NGC 5195 companion to reproduce its grand-design spiral structure and current interacting configuration.

quotes.quote: This particular calculation was designed to model M 51, and is described in detail in Dobbs2010 . The galaxy is set up in the same way as the flocculent model, but is then placed on an orbit with a perturbing galaxy of 1/3 the mass. The orbits of the two galaxies are taken from Theis2003 , who performed N-body calculations designed to reproduce M 51, and the initial locations and velocities of the galaxies are stated in Dobbs2010 .

The perturbing galaxy NGC 5195 is represented by a point mass in our calculations. The tidal perturbation in the interacting case leads to a grand design m=2 spiral structure (Fig. 1). The nominal time of 301 Myr represents the closest the simulation got to modelling the current picture of M 51 (or NGC 5194) and its companion NGC 5195.

sources.summary: The paper maps CO(1--0) across a spiral arm in M 51, resolving GMC-scale clumps and arguing that smooth GMAs form at arms before star formation in their cores.

quotes.quote: We present sensitive and high angular resolution CO(1--0) data obtained by the Combined Array for Research in Millimeter-wave Astronomy (CARMA) observations toward the nearby grand-design spiral galaxy M 51. The angular resolution of 0.7'' corresponds to 30 pc, which is similar to the typical size of Giant Molecular Clouds (GMCs), and the sensitivity is also high enough to detect typical GMCs. Within the 1' field of view centered on a spiral arm, a number of GMC-scale structures are detected as clumps.

However, only a few clumps are found to be associated with each Giant Molecular Association (GMA), and more than 90 of the total flux is resolved out in our data. Considering the high sensitivity and resolution of our data, these results indicate that GMAs are not mere confusion of GMCs but plausibly smooth structures. In addition, we have found that the most massive clumps are located downstream of the spiral arm, which suggests that they are at a later stage of molecular cloud evolution across the arm and plausibly are cores of GMAs.

sources.summary: The paper maps the nearby M51 galaxy system in radio and infrared, finding that the radio-IR correlation varies across spiral arms, interarm and outer regions, and the central disk.

quotes.quote: In this paper we investigate the radio continuum emission and its correlation with the IR emission within M51 at high spatial resolution, down to 200 pc. Throughout this paper, M51 refers to the interacting galaxy pair while M51a and M51b refer, respectively, to NGC5194 and the companion NGC5195. The aim of this study is to probe on what spatial scales the radio-IR correlation holds and the role played by the environment for this relation (e.g. arm/interarm regions).

In this paper we presented high quality radio continuum data of the nearby galaxy system M51 at three frequencies, 1.4 GHz (20 cm), 4.9 GHz (6 cm) and 8.4 GHz (3.6 cm), at a resolution between 1.5 \ and 2.4 . Combining these radio data with deconvolved Spitzer IR images, we were able to investigate the radio-IR correlation within M51 on spatial scales down to about 200 pc. The wavelet analysis of the radio and IR maps of M51 reveals that the radio-IR correlation is not uniform accros the galactic disk and exhibits a complex behavior due to the different galactic structures present in the galactic disk.

sources.summary: The paper uses M51 as an exponential gas disk example, adopting its scale radius and kinematics and predicting a radial CFE decline from central regions outward.

quotes.quote: This is a reasonable description of the ISM in M51 when taking R_ d =3 kpc schuster07 , and therefore we set the other parameters to values that are appropriate for that galaxy. We consider central surface densities of _ g (0)= 10,30,100 pc ^ 2 , where the highest value is representative of M51. The circular velocity is taken to be v_ =200 km s ^ 1 rand93 and is converted to an angular velocity profile using the M51 scale radius of R_ d =3 kpc. The adopted gas velocity dispersion is _ g =8 km s ^ 1 schuster07 .

As shown in the right-hand panel of Figure fig:spatial , the CFE covers a broad range, hitting =30 in the central parts of M51 to =3 at R_ gc =10 kpc 3R_ d . Like in the preceding cases, the CFE profiles shown here assume a flat rotation curve and no radial variation of the gas velocity dispersion.

sources.summary: The paper contrasts M51 with NGC 6946, describing overlapping and offset magnetic and material arms, possible multiple spiral patterns, and inner-galaxy downstream displacement.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, the mutual arrangement of magnetic and material arms is more complicated in M51 PFSBBFH06,fetal11 where the two patterns overlap in some regions but are systematically offset by about 0.5-- 0.6 elsewhere adopting 7.6 for the distance to M51 -- CFJKLD02 . This linear displacement corresponds to the angular phase difference of 5-- 10^ at =3 - 6 where such an offset is best pronounced. The two arms visible in polarized emission at 6.2 intersect the material arms observed in other tracers at =5 - 6 .

ESE89 suggest that M51 has two spiral arm systems, with the co-rotation radii at =4.8 and =12 . GCG93 determine the co-rotation radius to be 5.8 from CO observations and modelling of M51. Then the region at =3 - 6 is (mostly) inside the co-rotation radius and the magnetic arms in the inner galaxy are displaced downstream of the material arms, as expected in our model.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes CO brightness and integrated-intensity PDFs across M51, finding environmental variations, lognormal global behavior, and bright-emission excesses in spiral arms and the central region.

quotes.quote: We analyse the distribution of CO brightness temperature and integrated intensity in M51 at 40 pc resolution using new \ data from the Plateau de Bure Arcsecond Whirlpool Survey (PAWS). We present probability distribution functions (PDFs) of the CO emission within the PAWS field-of-view, which covers the inner 11 7 kpc of M51. We find clear variations in the shape of CO PDFs both within different M51 environments, defined according to dynamical criteria, and between M51 and two nearby low-mass galaxies, M33 and the Large Magellanic Cloud.

Globally, the PDFs for the inner disk of M51 can be represented by narrow lognormal functions that cover 1 to 2 orders of magnitude in CO brightness and integrated intensity. The PDFs for the interarm region are approximately lognormal, but in the spiral arms and central region of M51, they exhibit diverse shapes with a significant excess of bright CO emission. For galaxies with strong spiral structure such as M51, our results indicate that galactic-scale dynamical processes play a significant role in the formation and evolution of GMCs and stellar clusters.

sources.summary: The paper uses M51 to discuss H II-region extinction, source blending, and consistency between H-alpha and Pa luminosity-size measurements.

quotes.quote: The visual extinctions of individual H ii regions in the almost face-on spirals like M51 can reach values as high as A_V 6 mag Scoville01 , implying that the H line is dimmed by two orders of magnitude, but Pa only a factor of 2.

However, Scoville01 find a L_ H D^2 relation for M51, even when using the 0.1 \ resolution HST images (corresponding to a linear scale of 4.5 pc at the distance of M51). Those authors thus argue that source blending is still strong in M51, despite the high angular resolution of the data.

Using HIIphot again, this time on the entire FoVs of the HST H and Pa images of M51a, to reconstruct the L - D relation, we find best-fit power indices _ H =2.47 0.04 for the entire covered disk with a diameter of 8 kpc, and _ Pa =2.37 0.07 , consistent with each other within 1 . We thus recover a steeper slope than either Scoville01 , who derive _ H =2.1 , and Gutierrez11 , who find _ H =1.924 . Our relation also shows significantly more scatter than that found by both Scoville01 and Gutierrez11 (both use similar algorithms to identify and recover H ii regions), but similar to the scatter recovered by Lee11 , who use HIIphot (but derives _ H =2.16 0.02 ). Clearly, the employed algorithm for identifying and measuring the H ii regions has an effect on the level of scatter recovered in the L - D relation. When limiting our analysis to the central 5 kpc diameter of M51 (the largest area available for our 12 program galaxies), we find an H L - D relation with _ H =2.62 0.07 . This value is consistent with our Pa results, that yield an average exponent =2.70 0.27 , implying consistency between our Pa analysis and the H analysis of Scoville01 . We conclude that although our Pa data are shallower than the M51 H by a factor of about 10 Kennicutt07 , the derived statistical properties of H ii regions are consistent with each other.

sources.summary: The PAWS paper maps GMCs in M51, showing environmental variation across the inner disk, spiral arms, inter-arm zones, and nuclear bar, with many clouds likely unbound.

quotes.quote: Using data from the PdBI Arcsecond Whirlpool Survey (PAWS), we have generated the largest extragalactic Giant Molecular Cloud (GMC) catalog to date, containing 1,507 individual objects. GMCs in the inner M51 disk account for only 54 of the total ^ 12 CO(1-0) luminosity of the survey, but on average they exhibit physical properties similar to Galactic GMCs. We do not find a strong correlation between the GMC size and velocity dispersion, and a simple virial analysis suggests that 30 of GMCs in M51 are unbound.

The morphology of the grand-design spiral galaxy M51 is ideal for studying the properties of the molecular gas in different galactic environments. Within the PAWS field there are three main regions where the molecular gas is likely subject to distinct physical conditions (see Fig. mask ), i.e. within the strong, nearly symmetric spiral arms , the inter-arm region situated upstream and downstream of the spiral arms and the central region , where the gas is influenced by the presence of a central elliptical concentration of old stars in the form of a nuclear bar ( rix93 ).

In summary, our finding that the properties of GMCs in M51 vary with galactic environment argues against the view that GMCs are long-lived, quasi-equilibrium entities, with a constant mass surface density and isolated from their interstellar environment.

sources.summary: The paper uses M51 as an external-galaxy comparison for spiral-arm molecular clouds, noting PAWS resolution, higher dense-gas fractions in arm clouds, and complex inner two-arm structure.

quotes.quote: The tendency of spiral arm clouds to have higher DGMFs would be consistent with what is found in M51 by Hughes2013 . They found that molecular clouds within the spiral arms have a higher fraction of gas at high densities than inter-arm clouds.

For example, the PAWS survey of M51 Schinnerer2013 represents the current state-of-the-art in extragalactic studies, reaching 40 pc resolution and a mass sensitivity of 1.2 10 ^5 M _ Colombo2014a . If the GMFs resided in M51, most would be resolved and detectable.

For an illustrative example, one need not look further than the two-arm Whirlpool galaxy, M51, whose structure shows high complexity in the inner few kiloparsecs Aalto1999, Schinnerer2013 . Since the spiral arms are closer to one another and the kinematics are more complex in the inner regions, assigning inter-arm clouds to one particular arm is much more difficult.

sources.summary: The paper uses M51 as a worked example of a sublinear Kennicutt-Schmidt relation, linking its changing depletion time to a thick diffuse molecular disk that contributes nearly half the CO luminosity.

quotes.quote: Using \ observations of M51 at 170 pc scales, Blanc+09 infer a sublinear KS relationship, \ = 0.82 0.05.

In M51, \ = 0.75, so \ decreases by a factor of 2 between 10 100 , since \ is constant, and \ varies from 1.7 to 2.5 Gyr. Now, if free-fall collapse at typical GMC densities 100 \ govern , then \ is only a fraction of a percent (0.1 0.2 in M51) of the total molecular content.

Additionally, recent combined interferometric and single-dish CO observations of M51 have unveiled a thick disk of diffuse molecular gas Schinnerer+13, Pety+13, Hughes+13 . This diffuse component accounts for nearly 50 of the detected CO luminosity Given the limited physical resolution of these extra-galactic observations, these diffuse gas fractions are likely lower limits. . This large contribution from the diffuse component in M51 certainly affects the derived sublinear KS relationship, with \ 0.7 0.8 SKB13 Blanc+09 .

sources.summary: The paper explains M51’s anomalous outer rotation curve as a warped disk whose inclination changes sharply, producing apparent counter-rotation and matching tidal-interaction simulations.

quotes.quote: Institute of Astronomy, The University of Tokyo, Mitaka, 181-0015 Tokyo, Email: sofue@ioa.s.u-tokyo.ac.jp galaxies: general --- galaxies: structure --- galaxies: individual, M51 --- galaxies: kinematics and dynamics We revisit the anomaly of rotation curve in the nearly face-on galaxy M51 that shows an apparently faster decrease of rotation velocity than the Keplerian law in the outer disk, further showing apparent counter rotation in the outermost HI disk. We interpret this anomaly as due to warping of the galactic disk, and determined the warping structure of M51's disk using the tilted-ring method, assuming that the intrinsic rotation curve is normal. It is shown that the disk is nearly flat in the inner disk at a constant inclination angle, but the disk suddenly bends at radius 7.5 kpc by about 27 .

In this short note, we revisit the anomaly of apparent rotation curve of M51, and interpret it as due to warping of the disk. Apparent bend of rotation curve at 7.5 kpc Figure rcM51 shows a rotation curve of M51 obtained by Sofue (1996) from compilation of observations in the H , CO and HI line emissions.
The here obtained warping structure agrees well with a numerical simulation of tidal interaction of M51 and NGC 5195 by Dobbs et al.

sources.summary: The review uses M51 as a key tidally interacting spiral, citing models where NGC 5195 orbits drive chaotic dynamics, arm kinks, merger evolution, radial flows, and short-timescale arm motion.

quotes.quote: For M51, the best matched orbit of M51 and NGC5195 currently involves two orbits of the NGC 5195 around M51, after which the two galaxies merge Salo2000b, Theis2003 . Consequently the dynamics are quite chaotic, whilst the orbit induces different sets of spiral arms resulting in noticeable kinks along the spiral arms as observed today Salo2000, Dobbs+2010 . The spiral arms consequently show clear departures from logarithmic spirals.

The simulations do predict that unlike the quasi-steady state spiral galaxy, the gas and stars are not found to be offset from each other. In their model of M51, Dobbs+2010 also showed that the double, and relatively close passage of the perturbing galaxy introduced somewhat chaotic dynamics, inducing large radial inflow and outflow motions (see also Shetty+2007 ). The chaotic dynamics also mean that the spiral arms may move with respect to the gas on relatively short ( 10 Myr) timescales.

sources.summary: The paper identifies M51 as the interacting spiral whose arm lies before NGC 5195, tying absorption velocities, H I emission, high molecular fraction, and turbulence to its gas.

quotes.quote: The grand design spiral galaxy M51 (NGC 5194) has long been known to be interacting with its companion, the SB0 galaxy NGC 5195, which is seen in projection behind dusty interstellar gas now understood to be associated with the northeastern spiral arm of M51. Spinrad (1973) commented on the obvious dust lanes that are seen in front of NGC 5195, and speculated that some of these could be due to foreground absorption from the outer arm of M51. However, it was Van Dyk (1987) who confirmed this speculation, discovering anomalously strong Na i D lines attributable to interstellar gas in the arm of M51 projected onto the nucleus of NGC 5195.

The M51 components were found to span a velocity range of over 140 km s ^ 1 , making them similar to intermediate-velocity clouds observed in the halo of the Milky Way. Yet many of the M51 components toward SN 2011dh, even those at extreme positive and negative velocities, were found to have Na i /Ca ii column density ratios of 1.0. Such values are typically associated with relatively cool quiescent gas in the disk of a galaxy like M51 or the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper surveys M51 with wide-field VLBI, describing its interacting Whirlpool system, star-forming disk, Seyfert nucleus, compact radio detections, supernova rates, and background AGNs.

quotes.quote: The target for this survey is the Whirlpool galaxy, (aka M51a, NGC 5194), that is currently undergoing an interaction with a smaller companion, M51b (aka NGC 5195). At a distance of 8.4 0.7 Mpc Vinkoetal12 and with a nearly face-on orientation, M51 is ideal for population and morphology studies at all wavelengths. Like most spiral galaxies M51a is actively forming stars predominantly (but not exclusively) within its spiral arms Calzettietal05,KS10 , with a slightly enhanced formation of young, massive star clusters towards M51b KS10 . Furthermore, in the last 70 years M51a and M51b have hosted four optically observed SNe: the Type Ia SN 1945A KS1971 ; the Type Ib/ SN 1994I Puckett1994 ; the Type Ib/ SN 2005cs Muendlein2005 ; and the Type IIb SN 2011dh Griga2011 .

The full disk of M51 was surveyed using the multi-phase centre technique (192 phase centres) for compact radio sources. By combining this single pointing of the EVN, with multi-wavelength radio observations, X-ray and optical data we investigated: the properties of the nuclear region of M51a (including a pc-scaled jet); the light curves of the Type IIb supernovae, SN 2011dh (radio) and the Type Ib/ supernova, SN 1994I (radio and x-ray); the nature of four background AGNs (J133005+471035, J133011+471041, J132932+471123, J133016+471024); and the supernova and star formation rates of M51a.

sources.summary: The paper models M 51 as a face-on interacting Sc spiral whose observed pattern speed is marginal for a stellar disk but consistent with a stable wave when gas is included.

quotes.quote: The exponential disc scale-length is measured as 4.0 kpc GBP99 M 51 (NGC 5194) This is a face-on spiral galaxy of the Hubble type Sc and is located at a distance of 9.6 Mpc SanTamm75 , in close interaction with NGC 5195. The pattern speed of the spiral structure is measured to be 38 km s ^ 1 kpc ^ 1 by Zimm04 , using CO as a tracer and by applying the TW method. The rotation curve for M 51 is found to be steeply rising up to R = 25'' and then for R > 25'' it saturates to 210 km s ^ 1 RAND93 , where 1''=46.5 pc, assuming a distance of 9.6 Mpc SanTamm75 .

For M 51, the stars-alone case barely supports a stable density wave when Q_ s = 1.5, but if it is set to a value larger than 1.5, then the stars-alone case no longer supports a stable density wave for the observed pattern speed.

3 there JS84a , but here we have kept constant as typical for a given Hubble type. .32 .32 .32 .32 .32 .32 .32 .32 .32 Fig. fig4 , and Fig. fig6 show that for NGC 6946 and M 51, the |s|_ obs value lies above the |s|_ cut-off value for the star-gas system, thus the inequality given by equation (8) is satisfied, and this is true for the whole range of parameter space considered here. Thus the stars plus gas case allows a stable solution.

sources.summary: The paper groups M51 with unbarred galaxies, fits disk and halo magnetic components separately, uses turbulent broadening estimates, and says the model reasonably reproduces its pitch angles.

quotes.quote: Group I consists of M31, M33, and M51, galaxies without a prominent bar, for which azimuthal Fourier components ( m=0 , m=1 , and m=2 ) have been fitted to the multi-frequency data on the synchrotron polarization angle in the galactic disk. Following Vaneck+15 , we compare our dynamo model with the axisymmetric ( m=0 ) component. For M51 and NGC 253, disk and halo magnetic field components were fitted separately Fletcher+11,Heesen+09b , while for M31, such a separation was considered but found to be unnecessary Fletcher+04 .

Observations of nonthermal H 1 broadening suggest, for the one-dimensional velocity dispersion in the radial ranges considered here, =(12 , 12 , 12 , 11) in M31 C. Carignan, private communication Chemin+09 , =(10 , 9) in M33 (C. Carignan, private communication), and =(22 , 22 , 21 , 20) in M51 Tamburro+09 . The corresponding values of the three-dimensional turbulent speed u are 3 times larger if the turbulence is isotropic. For the galaxies M31, M51, NGC 253, NGC 1566, NGC 6946, and IC 342, our model does a fairly reasonable job of reproducing magnetic pitch angles.

sources.summary: The paper targets M51 as a grand-design interacting spiral, measuring gas-star offsets in its arms using HI, CO, and stellar-mass maps to test galactic shock predictions.

quotes.quote: In order to investigate if this mechanism is at work in the grand-design spiral galaxy M51, we have measured azimuthal offsets between the peaks of stellar mass and gas mass distributions in its two spiral arms. The stellar mass distribution is created by the spatially resolved spectral energy distribution fitting to optical and near infrared images, while the gas mass distribution is obtained by high-resolution CO and HI data. For the inner region ( 150'' ), we find that one arm is consistent with the galactic shock while the other is not.

A prototypical grand-design spiral galaxy, M51 (or NGC 5194), is selected as a target because the arm to interarm contrast has been known to be large at a wide range of wavelength. The large contrast in stellar potential is necessary to form shock waves while that in gas density indicates the shock likely exists. This galaxy is also known as the whirlpool galaxy and is interacting with a companion galaxy, M51b or NGC 5195.

sources.summary: The paper presents new VLA S-band polarization observations of M51, using multi-frequency depolarization models to probe disk, halo, and transition-region magnetic-field properties.

quotes.quote: An excellent laboratory for studying large scale magnetic fields is the grand design face-on spiral galaxy M51. Due to wavelength-dependent Faraday depolarization, linearly polarized synchrotron emission at different radio frequencies gives a picture of the galaxy at different depths: Observations at L-band (1 - 2 GHz) probe the halo region while at C- and X-band (4 - 8 GHz) the linearly polarized emission probe the disk region of M51. We present new observations of M51 using the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (VLA) at S-band (2 - 4 GHz), where previously no polarization observations existed, to shed new light on the transition region between the disk and the halo. We discuss a model of the depolarization of synchrotron radiation in a multilayer magneto-ionic medium and compare the model predictions to the multi-frequency polarization data of M51 between 1 - 8 GHz.

Our study shows that the initial model parameters, i.e. the total regular and turbulent magnetic field strengths in the disk and halo of M51, need to be adjusted to successfully fit the models to the data. polarization, Galaxies: spiral, individual (M51), magnetic fields M51 is a nearby face-on grand design spiral galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses M 51 as the nearby star-forming disk template, citing its molecular-gas CO luminosity, distance, gas mass, well-studied rotation curve, and BIMA SONG data cube.

quotes.quote: We adopt as a spatial template the CO 1-0 emission from the nearby star forming disk galaxy, M 51. M 51 is one of the best studied galaxies in molecular gas sch13 . M 51 is at a distance of 7.6 Mpc (recessional velocity = 463 km/s), with an integrated CO 1-0 luminosity of L'_ CO = 1.6 10^ 9 K km/s pc ^2 , implying a molecular gas mass of 5.5 10^9 ( /3.4 ) M_ , where = 3.4 , corresponding to the Galactic conversion factor for CO 1-0 luminosity to total molecular gas mass.

We give the galaxy properties for M 51 and the higher redshift models in Table 1. The rotation curve of M 51 is well studied, and the numbers are given in our comparitive dynamical analysis below. We employ the public BIMA SONG CO 1-0 data cubes hel03 , as the starting point of the models.

sources.summary: The paper presents a complete SOFIA [C ii] map of M51, comparing star formation and infrared emission across its disk and noting interaction with companion M51b.

quotes.quote: Pineda Jorge.Pineda@jpl.nasa.gov We present a [ C ii ] 158 m map of the entire M51 (including M51b) grand--design spiral galaxy observed with the FIFI--LS instrument on SOFIA. We compare the [ C ii ] emission with the total far--infrared (TIR) intensity and star formation rate (SFR) surface density maps (derived using H and 24 m emission) to study the relationship between [ C ii ] and the star formation activity in a variety of environments within M51 on scales of 16 \ corresponding to 660 pc.
We find that the SFR, and [ C ii ] and TIR luminosities in M51 are dominated by the extended emission in M51's disk.

The velocity--resolved spectral map of M51 will be presented in a separate paper with emphasis on the gas kinematics. M51 is a nearby grand design spiral at a distance of 8.5 Mpc McQuinn2016 , with an inclination angle of 24 \ Daigle2006 . It is interacting with a smaller companion galaxy, M51b , classified as a barred lenticular (SB0 pec; Sandage1981 ) and a LINER galaxy Ho1997 .

sources.summary: The paper recovers M 51's known plumes and argues that its smooth, unusually shaped outer envelope is likely a flattened disk-like projection rather than a classical halo.

quotes.quote: Our observations reproduce previously reported low surface brightness structures, including extended plumes in M 51, and a newly discovered tidally extended dwarf galaxy in NGC 7331. Low surface brightness structures, or ``envelopes'', exceeding 50 kpc in diameter are found mostly in galaxies with M_V<-20.5 , and classic interaction signatures are infrequent. Defining a halo diameter at the surface brightness 28 mag/arcsec ^2 , we find that halo diameter is correlated with total galaxy luminosity.

M 51 presents a smooth but unusually shaped outer envelope, whereas M 74 and M 101 appear to show the more commonly seen spiral structure. In the case of M 83, the outer envelope is unusual in that it extends substantially beyond the disk, shows no spiral structure even in subtraction, and is oval and off-centred. This outermost structural feature suggests that (as is the case with M 51) its envelope is more likely to be a flattened disk-like projection.

sources.summary: M51 is included as a massive spiral where central and inner-region molecular clouds are in high-pressure environments and appear nearly self-virialized.

quotes.quote: Our galaxy sample comprises eight local galaxies spanning a wide range of stellar mass and morphology. We include one dwarf irregular: the LMC, two low-mass spirals: M33 and NGC300, two massive spirals: the Milky Way and M51, one starburst galaxy center: NGC253, one green-valley galaxy: M31, and one lenticular galaxy: NGC4526. We compile global measurements of the stellar mass ( M_ ), atomic ( M_ atom ) and molecular gas mass ( M_ mol ), and star formation rate (SFR) from the literature or we derive them from our own data. Our sample covers about two orders of magnitude in various host galaxy properties: M_ = 2 10^9 - 10^ 11 , SFR = 0.03 - 4.2 , sSFR = 2 10^ 12 2 10^ 10 yr ^ 1 , and f_ gas = M_ gas / (M_ gas + M_ ) = 0.01 - 0.5 .

To first order, ``self-gravitating'' clouds have high surface densities ( _ gmc 10^2 10^4 ) and are found in high ambient pressure environments ( P_ ext 4 10^4 - 7 10^7 K cm ^ 3 ) as in the central and inner Milky Way, M51, NGC253, and NGC4526. Despite the high ambient pressure in their environments, these clouds appear nearly virialized considering only their self-gravity and internal motions.

sources.summary: The review contrasts early long-lifetime claims for M51 molecular clouds with later 20-40 Myr GMC lifetimes, noting M51 remains the longest-lived case in one sample.

quotes.quote: Early CO observations of the nearby spiral galaxy M51 revealed the presence of a molecular cloud population between the spiral arms of the galaxy, suggesting that these structures are not transient but rather live for 100 Myr e.g. Scoville1979 . This cloud longevity, greatly in excess of the free-fall times of such structures (approximately a few Myr), suggests the existence of support against gravitational collapse by turbulence or magnetic fields e.g. Fleck1980, Shu1987, Krumholz2006 .
Using evolutionary streamlines, Meidt2015 find a GMC lifetime of 20--30 Myr in M51, again consistent with the measurement of 26--40 Myr obtained for the same galaxy by Chevance2019 .

The most compelling galaxy for which long ( 100 Myr) GMC lifetimes had been reported in the literature has traditionally been M51 Scoville1979,koda09 . While it is now clear that part of the disagreement with other studies e.g. Meidt2015 comes from the fact that the long timescale likely refers to a molecule lifetime rather than a GMC lifetime, there is also an important environmental factor. In the sample studied by Chevance2019 , M51 is the galaxy with the longest GMC lifetime, even though it is only 30 Myr on average -- a factor of three shorter than the inferred molecule lifetime.

sources.summary: The paper models M51's central diffuse X-ray spectrum, linking charge exchange emission to jet-driven outflow interacting with neutral circumnuclear gas.

quotes.quote: Here we present a case study of the circumnuclear region of the grand design spiral galaxy M51 (also known as the Whirlpool galaxy; d 8.58 Mpc, mcquinn2016distance ). Unlike the edge-on galaxy M82, where the inner nuclear region is blocked by the disk, more emission from the circumnuclear ISM can be observed in M51, because it is a nearly face-on galaxy inclination angle: 20^ ; hu2013new . M51 is considered as a ``quiescently star-forming galaxy,'' with a mean SF rate density 0.015 M_ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 calzetti2005star , although this value can be an order of magnitude higher in its central region leroy2017cloudscale .

We use a model including a thermal and a CX components to describe the high-resolution XMM-Newton/RGS spectrum of the diffuse emission in the central region of M51. Representative signatures of CX emission -- especially the prominent \ forbidden line and the excess emission in the \ Ly lines -- can be well explained by the model. Combined with the \ images in the \ and the \ bands, we find the soft X-ray emission is dominated by the jet-driven outflow and its interaction with the ambient neutral material.

sources.summary: The paper detects 293 M51 globular-cluster candidates and contrasts its stricter HST selection with a much larger prior M51 star-cluster catalogue.

quotes.quote: Our search, which surpasses the fractional area covered by all previous searches in these galaxies, has resulted in the detection of 158 GC candidates in M81, 1123 in M101, 226 in NGC 4258, 293 in M51 and 173 in NGC 628. The LFs constructed from this dataset, after correcting for relatively small contamination from reddened young clusters, are log-normal in nature, which was hitherto established only for the Milky Way (MW) and Andromeda among spiral galaxies.

M 51: Star clusters in M51 were catalogued by Hwang:2008 using HST/ACS images. They used the SExtractor parameters stellarity, FWHM and ellipticity for star cluster selection. They created two subsamples, one with 2.4 < FWHM < 20 and the other with 2.4 < FWHM < 40 pixel, resulting in a catalogue of 8400 stellar cluster candidates. Only 85 of these objects are in our catalogue of 223 GCs.

sources.summary: The paper applies a high-resolution extinction-mapping method to M51/NGC 5194, adopting its distance and inclination, excluding NGC 5195, and estimating pixel-level dust properties.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we showcase our new high-resolution extinction mapping technique for our test galaxy M51 (NGC 5194), chosen for its ample survey coverage, its relatively near distance, and its near face-on inclination. We adopt 7.6 Mpc as the M51 distance to the MW ciardullo_planetary_2002 , and an inclination of 22^ tully_kinematics_1974, colombo_pdbi_2014_moment .

For the purpose of this paper, as a proof of concept, we are only interested in applying our new technique to NGC 5194 (referred to here as simply M51). Its companion, NGC 5195, is therefore cut from the original HST observations since it is known to have different metallicity and dust-to-gas mass ratio than M51 e.g. mentuch_cooper_spatially_2012 .

The pixel-by-pixel SED fitting used non-linear least squares to estimate the dust temperatures ( T_d ) and dust emission surface densities ( _ em ) in each pixel of M51. The reference absorption coefficient adopted was _ 250 m =21.6 cm ^2 g ^ 1 from hereafter OH94 ossenkopf_dust_1994 , often used in MW studies e.g. kauffmann_mambo_2008,2009_schuller_atlasgal , which is valid for grains covered by a thick ice mantle that follow the classical Mathis, Rumpl, and Nordsieck (MRN) size distribution model mathis_size_1977 . We assume a fixed dust emissivity spectral index of =2 an appropriate assumption for metal-rich, late-type galaxies such as M51 see boselli_far-infrared_2012,mentuch_cooper_spatially_2012, clark_empirical_2016 .

sources.summary: The paper applies HIIphot to M51, detecting 1229 H II regions, measuring the H II-region luminosity function, finding fainter interarm regions, and estimating a diffuse emission fraction of about 0.45.

quotes.quote: We present results for the well-studied, nearby spiral M51 in which 1229 H 2 regions are detected above the 5 level. A simple, weighted power-law fit to the measured H luminosity function ( H 2 LF) above log L _ H = 37.6 gives = -1.75 0.06 , despite a conspicuous break in the H 2 LF observed near L _ H = 10^ 38.9 . Our best-fit slope is marginally steeper than measured by Rand (1992), perhaps reflecting our increased sensitivity at low luminosities and to notably diffuse objects.

H 2 regions located in interarm gaps are preferentially less luminous than counterparts which constitute M51's grand-design spiral arms and are best fit with a power-law slope of = -1.96 0.15 . We assign arm/interarm status for H 2 regions based upon the varying surface brightness of diffuse emission as a function of position throughout the image. Using our measurement of the integrated flux contributed by resolved H 2 regions in M51, we estimate the diffuse fraction to be approximately 0.45 -- in agreement with the determination of Greenawalt et al. (1998).

sources.summary: The paper treats M51 as a high-SFR test case for cluster mass limits, using 1052 clusters and finding a lack of old massive clusters plus evidence for a truncated cluster mass function.

quotes.quote: A good candidate galaxy, which is big and has a high SFR, is M51. We used the 1052 star clusters identified by to study the SoS relation of with log(age). In Fig. fig:1 we show a comparison between the clusters in the LMC (left), the SMC (middle) and M51 (right).
In M51, however, there is a lack of old ( > 10^8 yr), massive ( > 10^6 ) clusters.

The brightest cluster vs. log(age) in M51 follow this fading line of a 5 10^5 cluster quite well, similar to what was found for the ``Antennae'' galaxies (for a 10^6 cluster) . This suggests that the cluster mass function in M51 and the ``Antennae'' galaxies is truncated around 0.5 1.0 10^6 .

sources.summary: The paper examines M51 outer-disk infrared dust emission associated with H I and finds outer-disk star formation efficiencies far below those in the bright inner disk.

quotes.quote: We examine faint infrared emission features detected in Spitzer Space Telescope images of M51, which are associated with atomic hydrogen in the outer disk and tidal tail at R R _ 25 (4.9 , 14 kpc at d=9.6 Mpc). The infrared colors of these features are consistent with the colors of dust associated with star formation in the bright disk. However, the star formation efficiency (as a ratio of star formation rate to neutral gas mass) implied in the outer disk is lower than that in the bright disk of M51 by an order of magnitude, assuming a similar relationship between infrared emission and star formation rate in the inner and outer disks.

For the outer disk positions of M51, the L(IR) SFEs are 0.04-0.2 Gyr ^ 1 , and the H SFEs are 0.02-0.06 Gyr ^ 1 . The median SFE for the 10 outer disk positions where significant emission was detected in H , 8 , and 24 \ emission is 0.04 and 0.07 Gyr ^ 1 from H (corrected) and L(IR), respectively. For the inner disk positions of M51, the L(IR) SFEs are 0.8-4 Gyr ^ 1 , and the H SFEs are 0.6-3 Gyr ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper measures M 51 disk, arm, and interarm scale-lengths, finding stronger wavelength-dependent scale-length changes in arms, attributed to greater dust extinction there.

quotes.quote: M 51 In Fig. 4 (a) we show azimuthally averaged radial surface brightness profiles for the whole galaxy in the B , R , I , and K bands, in Fig. 4 (b) the corresponding profiles for the arm regions of the disk, and in Fig. 4 () the profiles for the interarm zones. The best fit to the exponential disk component is indicated in each figure. The fitted values of the observed disk scale-length are listed in Table 1 for each photometric band, as well as the limits of the radial ranges over which the fits were obtained.

If we look at the scale-lengths for the disk as a whole, we see a modest change between the B and the K bands: a fall of some 25 , but this masks a major difference between the arms and the inter-arm zones. In the arms the B scale-length is longer than that in K by a factor of 1.6, whereas in the interarm disk the scale-lengths change very little over the whole wavelength range. This stronger trend of scale-length variation with wavelength in the arms almost certainly results from more dust there than in the inter-arm zones, and we will examine this below using our theoretical models for comparison.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M51 as an X-ray comparison case, noting emission outside the optical limits, unresolved diffuse components, matching radio/X-ray scale lengths, and possible magnetic-reconnection heating.

quotes.quote: In M51 (Ehle, Pietsch and Beck, 1995) an X-ray emission comes from outside the optical limits and especially from the intergalactic region between M51 and its companion. This emission is hard to explain by the presence of binary system. As in the case of NGC 6946 a significant part of X-ray emission is not resolved into individual point sources. The authors argue that the diffuse emission can not be explained by a large number of unresolved point sources.

The scale lengths of the thermal radio continuum emission and of the diffuse X-ray emission are similar. Although it is suggested that a hot gas from starburst regions (transported via galactic fountains or winds) can be responsible for the diffuse emission, we point out that the Parker-shearing instability together with the magnetic reconnection can lead to the same effect.

sources.summary: The paper uses ACS imaging of M51 to measure cluster radii across its disc, reporting old globular-cluster candidates, faint fuzzy clusters, and weak cluster radius trends with environment.

quotes.quote: We use / \ observations of the spiral galaxy M51 in , \ and \ to select a large sample of star clusters with accurate effective radius measurements in an area covering the complete disc of M51. We present the dataset and study the radius distribution and relations between radius, colour, arm/interarm region, galactocentric distance, mass and age.

We find 70 clusters in our sample which have colours consistent with being old GC candidates and we find 6 new ``faint fuzzy'' clusters in, or projected onto, the disc of M51. The radius distribution can not be fitted with a power law similar to the one for star-forming clouds. We find an increase in \ with colour as well as a higher fraction of clusters with 0.05 in the interarm regions.

sources.summary: The review discusses M51's cluster disruption and luminosity function, describing a short central disruption timescale, good fit to a lower cutoff-mass LF model, and mild interaction with a companion.

quotes.quote: Evidence of a large IM fraction ( 70 ) has also been claimed in M51 (Bastian 2005 b ), but this may be partly due to age-dating artifacts around 10^7 years (Gieles 2009). In the SMC, Chandar (2006) derive an age distribution of N/ ^ 0.85 for <10^9 years, similar to that seen in the Antennae, but Gieles (2007) argue that this may be caused by fading below the detection limit at old ages and instead conclude that no significant MID is needed to explain the age distribution of SMC clusters (see also de Grijs Goodwin 2008 for evidence supporting the latter scenario). The cluster-formation efficiency In starburst and merger systems, young clusters often account for a large fraction (10--20 ) of the total blue/ultraviolet flux (Tremonti 2001; Meurer 1995; Zepf 1999; Fall 2005), consistent with essentially all stars forming in clusters (see also Johnson 2009). In the Milky Way, the majority of stars form in embedded clusters (Lada Lada 2003). However, most stars eventually end up belonging to the field. Observationally, it is difficult to tell whether all stars were born in clusters, with a large fraction dispersing almost immediately, or whether some stars were born in genuinely dispersed mode. For practical purposes, one may still define a cluster-formation `efficiency' as the ratio of the number of stars that end up in clusters relative to the field. Since this ratio will depend on age, ideally some age range should be specified. For optically visible clusters, Bastian (2008) finds a constant efficiency of 8 in galaxies spanning six orders of magnitude in star-formation rate (SFR). On the other hand, the fraction of the total U band light in galaxies originating from clusters correlates with the area-normalized SFR of the parent galaxy, ranging from well below 1 in quiescent systems to the high numbers found in starbursts (Larsen Richtler 2000). The near-absence of clusters in IC 1613, and the nonuniversal GC specific frequency (number of GCs per unit host-galaxy luminosity; Harris 1991, 2010), are other hints that variations in may exist. Even if a single indicator of star formation (e.g., the far-infrared or ultraviolet luminosity) is used, different systematic errors will affect galaxies differing in dust content, metallicity, ratio of current to past SFR and other parameters (Kennicutt 1998). Such systematic errors on the parent-galaxy SFRs are likely different than those associated with the cluster-formation rates, which are typically inferred from direct observations of cluster populations but still subject to uncertainties due to disruption, potential confusion with other objects and completeness effects. Consequently, remains difficult to constrain. Secular evolution Clusters that survive IM will continue to evolve dynamically on longer timescales as a result of internal two-body relaxation, external shocks and mass loss due to stellar evolution (Vesperini 2010). This `secular evolution' will lead to the gradual evaporation of any star cluster and, eventually, its total dissolution. Two-body relaxation causes the velocities of the stars in the cluster to approach a Maxwellian distribution and stars with velocities above the escape velocity will gradually evaporate from the cluster. The two-body relaxation time scales as t_ rel M R_ h ^ 3/2 , but the actual evaporation time, t_ ev , may scale nonlinearly with t_ rel (Baumgardt Makino 2003). In addition, external shocks will lead to dissolution on a timescale t_ sh M R_ h ^ 3 (Spitzer 1987). Shocks may be due to encounters with spiral arms, GMCs or, for GCs on eccentric orbits, passages through the galactic disc or near the bulge. Finally, stellar evolution causes mass loss as stars are turned into much less massive remnants. Over a 10 Gyr time span, about one third of the initial cluster mass is lost this way (e.g., Bruzual Charlot 2003). For many practical purposes, secular dissolution may be conveniently characterized by a single dissolution timescale t_ dis , such that mass is lost at a rate M/ t _ dis = -M/t_ dis . The dissolution timescale may be parameterized as t_ dis = t_4 (M/10^4 M _ )^ , where t_4 10^9 years and 0.65 for clusters in the solar neighbourhood (Lamers 2005). The relatively larger number of old clusters in the Magellanic Clouds suggests that disruption is less efficient there (Elson Fall 1985; Girardi 1995; Hodge 1987; de Grijs Anders 2006), while the disruption timescale in the central regions of M51 appears much shorter than in the solar neighbourhood (Boutloukos Lamers 2003).

It is clear that the M_ =2 10^5 M _ model LF matches the M51 data quite well, while the M_ =2 10^6 M _ LF is too shallow at the bright end. For the Antennae, however, the M_ =2 10^6 M _ LF provides a better fit. The Antennae LF flattens further below M_V 8 mag, but Whitmore (1999) indicate that the selection of cluster candidates becomes less reliable below this limit. M51 is perhaps not the most typical spiral, since it is mildly interacting with a nearby companion. However, the LF comparison suggests that the MF there is more similar to that in noninteracting spirals than in the merging Antennae galaxies. Figure fig:lfs shows that the LF is expected (and observed) to steepen towards the bright end, so that a single power law generally provides a poor fit over an extended magnitude range. This steepening has been noted in other data sets, including the merger NGC 3256 (Zepf 1999) and various spiral galaxies (Larsen 2002; Dolphin Kennicutt 2002). Typical power-law slopes are between 2.0 and 2.5 . From similar modelling of the LF, Gieles (2006 a ) inferred a truncation of the MF around M_ up 10^5 M _ in M51 and another spiral, NGC 6946, and it was suggested already by Zhang Fall (1999) that the steepening of the Antennae LF brighter than M_V 10 mag might indicate truncation of the MF around 10^6 M _ .

sources.summary: The thesis treats M51 as a grand-design interacting galaxy where tides have shaped tails and spirals, then tests compressive tidal regions in a simulation.

quotes.quote: However, other mergers deserve as much attention as them, like for instance the grand design galaxy M51 and its companion NGC 5195. In this object, the tidal field has already played a crucial role in the formation of large scale structures like tails and spirals. It is then reasonable to question whether the smaller scales are also affected by the tides and in which fashion.

The intruder NGC 5195 is a point whose mass is one third of the the one of M51. To address these points, I use the smoothed particles hydrodynamics simulation presented in Dobbs2010 to compute the tidal field and look for compressive regions.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes M51 with Chandra and 2MASS data, using blank-sky and local background handling and separating grand-design spiral arm and interarm regions whose soft X-ray/K-band ratios differ by no more than 30%.

quotes.quote: For galaxies significantly smaller than the field of view we estimated the background components using a combination of a number of regions away from galaxies. The angular size of four galaxies (M51, M74, M81, M83) exceeds or is comparable with the extent of the combined Chandra image, therefore we used the ACIS ``blank-sky'' files http://cxc.harvard.edu/contrib/maxim/acisbg/ to estimate the background.
The obtained 2MASS K-band images were background subtracted for all galaxies except for M51 and NGC3077, for these we estimated the background level by using several nearby regions off the galaxy.

Four galaxies in our sample (M51, M74 , M81, and M83) have grand design spiral arms allowing us to measure L_X/L_K ratios of spiral arms and interarm regions separately, obtained values are given in Table tab:lxlk . The arm and interarm regions were separated based on optical and near-infrared images. To our surprise, we did not find large difference between spirals arms and interarm regions, their L_X/L_K ratios being different by no more than 30 .

sources.summary: The paper models M51 as a face-on Whirlpool Galaxy whose disturbed HI disk constrains a massive companion, best fit by a 1:3 perturber with 15 kpc pericenter.

quotes.quote: The nearby Whirlpool Galaxy (M51) and its companion (NGC 5195) provide an useful test case for our method. Figure f:m51data shows a map of the atomic hydrogen (HI) surface density distribution of M51 from THINGS (The HI Nearby Galaxy Survey (Walter et al. 2008). At first sight, one notes the striking spiral structure in the inner regions, and the broad HI arm that extends far out and curves towards the east.

The best-fit simulation has a perturber mass ratio of 1:3 and a pericentric approach distance of 15 kpc. Furthermore, at the best-fit time, the perturber in the simulation is at a distance that is 1.5 times as large as the extent of the stellar spiral arms of M51, which is within a factor of 1.2 of the observed ratio.

sources.summary: The paper maps M51 along its major axis, reporting nuclear C18O and dense-gas detections, exponential CO and 13CO radial profiles, kinematic agreement, and central line-width dilution.

quotes.quote: We have also detected C ^ 18 O, CS J =2 1, and HCO ^+ J =1 0 emission in the nuclear regions of M82 and M51. These are the first systematical extragalactic detections of \ and its isotopes from the PMO 14m telescope. We have conducted half-beam spacing mapping of M82 over an area of 4' 2.5' and major axis mapping of NGC 3627, NGC 3628, NGC 4631, and M51. The radial distributions of \ and ^ 13 CO in NGC 3627, NGC 3628, and M51 can be well fitted by an exponential profile.
In NGC 3627 and M51, ^ 13 CO profiles follow similar exponential distributions as .

So combining the variations in line width shown in Fig. Fig5 , we believe the decrement of CO intensity in the central region of M51 is likely as a result of the dilution in velocity with much wider line width in the center than that in the outer regions of the central disk.

sources.summary: The review uses M 51 as a key example of strong spiral-arm magnetic fields, radio-infrared correlations, anisotropic ordered fields, and weak regular disk modes.

quotes.quote: Radio-faint galaxies like M 31 (Fig. fig:m31 ) and M 33 taba08 , our Milky Way's neighbors, have weaker total magnetic fields (about 6 G), while gas-rich spiral galaxies with high star-formation rates, like M 51 and NGC 6946 (Fig. fig:m51 ), have total field strengths of 20-30 G in their spiral arms. The exponent of the correlation in M 51 was found to be different in the central region, spiral arms and interarm regions dumas11 . In galaxies with strong density waves like M 51 fletcher11 enhanced ordered (anisotropic) fields occur at the inner edges of the inner optical arms and in the interarm regions (Fig. fig:m51 left).

If the beautiful spiral pattern of M 51 seen in radio polarization fletcher11 were only due to a regular field, its line-of sight component should generate a conspicuous large-scale pattern in Faraday rotation, which is not observed. A weak regular field (dynamo modes m = 0 and m = 1 ) also exists in the disk of M 51 fletcher11 .

sources.summary: The paper tests its NGC 891 model at M51's low inclination, matching most of M51's integrated SED by changing dust mass and old-disk luminosity, and linking FIR differences to interaction-driven star formation.

quotes.quote: With minor adjustment our SED models are able to match the majority of M51's SED, a similar galaxy at a near face-on different inclination.

We investigated the viability of our models at other projected inclinations by comparing them to M51 ( i 20 ; Figure 1). For simplicity we fix all geometric parameters (including clumping and spirality) of the dust and stellar distributions as well as L _2 , and allow only M _ d and L _1 to vary. A good match to the observed, spatially integrated SED (except at 24 m, as explained above) is found by increasing the dust mass by 75 and halving the old-disk luminosity (model 5). The FIR flux distributions indicate that M51 has a dearth of cold dust compared to N891 despite M51's increased dust mass, consistent with elevated star-formation induced by M51's ongoing interaction.

sources.summary: The paper describes M51 as a Seyfert 2 galaxy whose circumnuclear molecular gas is disturbed near the nucleus, lacking a clear rotating torus and likely affected by AGN jet activity.

quotes.quote: We therefore observed the circumnuclear molecular gas of M51 with 10 pc spatial resolution to spatially resolve the gas kinematics around the Seyfert 2 nucleus in this galaxy. We used the new-A (newly extended) configuration (maximum baseline length =760 m) of the IRAM Plateau de Bure Interferometer (PdBI), and observed with the CO(2-1) line, since the CO(2-1) line flux is higher than that of the CO(1-0) line, and it is possible to obtain higher spatial resolution images. Figure fig-co21 shows the newly obtained circumnuclear CO(2-1) image of M51 .

In summary, our 10 pc spatial resolution CO(2-1) results show that there is no clear evidence of rotating disk or torus around the Seyfert 2 nucleus of M51 as suggested in the past, but exhibit that there are highly disturbed molecular gas features very close to the nucleus, possibly affected by the AGN activities. Furthermore, the obscuring material of this type 2 AGN has the size much smaller than our beam size of 10 pc.

sources.summary: The paper reports resolved H-band polarimetry of M51, finding no detectable near-infrared polarization and ruling out a Serkowski-like wavelength dependence across its face.

quotes.quote: The galaxy M51 was observed using the Mimir instrument on the Perkins telescope to constrain the resolved H-band (1.6 m) polarization across the galaxy. These observations place an upper limit of P_H<0.05 on the H band polarization across the face of M51, at 0.6 arcsecond pixel sampling. Even with smoothing to coarser angular resolutions, to reduce polarization uncertainty, the H band polarization remains undetected. The polarization upper limit at H band, when combined with previous resolved optical polarimetry, rules out a Serkowski-like polarization dependence on wavelength. Other polarization mechanisms cannot account for the observed polarization ratio ( P_H/P VRI 0.05 ) across the face of M51.

sources.summary: The paper compares M51 GMCs with those in M33 and the LMC, finding M51 clouds larger, brighter, and more turbulent, with higher average molecular surface density.

quotes.quote: We compare the properties of giant molecular clouds (GMCs) in M51 identified by the Plateau de Bure Interferometer Whirlpool Arcsecond Survey (PAWS) with GMCs identified in wide-field, high resolution surveys of CO emission in M33 and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). We find that GMCs in M51 are larger, brighter and have higher velocity dispersions relative to their size than equivalent structures in M33 and the LMC. These differences imply that there are genuine variations in the average mass surface density _ H_ 2 of the different GMC populations. To explain this, we propose that the pressure in the interstellar medium surrounding the GMCs plays a role in regulating their density and velocity dispersion. We find no evidence for a correlation between size and linewidth in any of M51, M33 or the LMC when the CO emission is decomposed into GMCs, although moderately robust correlations are apparent when regions of contiguous CO emission (with no size limitation) are used.

sources.summary: The paper models M 51 as a nearby late-type spiral in an interacting system, using panchromatic radiative transfer to study its dust heating by young and old stellar populations.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we construct a 3D radiative transfer model of the grand-design spiral galaxy M 51 M 51 is a nearby, late-type (SAbc) galaxy located at a distance of D = 8.4 Mpc 1997ApJ...479..231F , and belongs to an interacting system with the companion galaxy NGC 5195. The galaxy pair is often referred to as M 51, while the individual galaxy NGC 5194 is named M 51a. In this paper, we refer to M 51 for the single galaxy NGC 5194 belonging to the interacting system. (NGC 5194) with a near to face-on orientation ( i 20 ^ ) and a huge database of multi-band ancillary data.

We exploit the panchromatic RT model of stars and dust in M 51 to analyze the main dust heating sources on spatially resolved scales across the infrared spectrum. Rather than giving hints on the predominant dust heating source, the RT calculations track the emission of stellar populations of different ages and their interaction with the dusty interstellar medium, which allows to constrain the percentage of dust heated by young stars ( 100 Myr) and the old stellar population ( 10 Gyr) at every location in 3D space across multiple infrared wavebands.

sources.summary: The paper compares M 51 with IC 342 through nonthermal-emission breaks, outer-disk scalelength behavior, diffusive propagation, and a weak regular field with strong anisotropic turbulence.

quotes.quote: LOFAR and VLA data of the spiral galaxy M 51 also reveal breaks in the radial distribution of the nonthermal emission at a radius of about 10 kpc, beyond which the star-forming activity decreases sharply mulcahy14 , suggesting a similarity of the underlying physical processes between M 51 and IC 342. However, the difference in exponential scalelengths at 20 between the inner and the outer disks is shorter in M 51 than in IC 342. Furthermore, the exponential scalelengths in the outer disk of M 51 have a frequency dependence that is weaker than in IC 342 and is consistent with diffusive propagation. basu13 studied the ratio of scalelengths between 90 and 20 in five spiral galaxies.
M 51 has a weak large-scale regular field but a strong anisotropic turbulent field fletcher11 that may support diffusive propagation along the field.
The spiral pattern in the gas of M 51 is also distorted and short-lived, so that only a weak large-scale regular field and a rudimentary magnetic arm could develop.

sources.summary: M 51 is treated as a Seyfert 2 AGN host whose central molecular abundances resemble starbursts more than the other AGNs, despite nuclear and spiral-arm comparisons.

quotes.quote: Among the galaxies in our sample, M 51, NGC 1068, and NGC 7469 host an AGN (the first two are classified as Sy 2, and the latter as Sy 1, Osterbrock93 ). The CNDs of these galaxies are surrounded by arms of active star forming regions at 1-2 kpc distance from their centres. Unfortunately, there are no similar estimations for NGC 7469 and M 51 calculated for the IRAM 30 m beam at the 3 mm wavelength of our survey. CH _3 OH is also detected in M 51 (not in NGC 7469), but with a clearly lower abundance than in NGC 1068. Interestingly, HCN/C ^ 18 O, CN/C ^ 18 O, C _2 H/C ^ 18 O, and HNC/C ^ 18 O in M 51 are closer to the starburst results than to the other AGNs (Fig. abundances ).

sources.summary: The paper applies its tidal-interaction models to M51/NGC 5195, matching the pair's mass ratio, pericenter distance, arm strength, and possible double-interaction history.

quotes.quote: While we employ very simple galaxy models for tidal interactions, our models can well be applied to the M51/NGC 5195 system that have the mass ratio of 0.3 - 0.55 (e.g., smi90 ) and a pericenter distance of 20 - 30 (e.g., sal00a ), corresponding to models TA1 and TA2. As Figure fig:F-R shows, the arm strength is maximized 200 after the pericenter passage. lee05 found observationally that the age distribution of star clusters in the arms of M51 has a narrow peak at 4 - 10 and a broad peak at 100 - 400 . If the enhanced star formation is really triggered by tidal effects, the M51/NGC 5195 pair might have undergone double interactions, the first one 400 - 500 ago and the second one 50 - 100 ago sal00a , considering the time delay between the arm formation and the closest approach. The peak arm strength in models TA1 and TA2 is 10-20 , consistent with the radially averaged value of 15-20 from K band observations (e.g., rix93,rix95,sal00b,sco01 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes M 51 as having a strong anisotropic turbulent field shaped by density-wave compression, a weaker regular disk field, a halo regular field, and a rudimentary magnetic arm.

quotes.quote: If the beautiful spiral pattern of M 51 seen in radio polarization (Fig. fig:m51 ) were only due to a regular field in the disk, its line-of sight component should generate a conspicuous large-scale pattern in RM, but this is not observed fletcher11 . This means that a large amount of the ordered field is anisotropic turbulent \ and probably generated by compression and shear of the non-axisymmetric gas flows in the density-wave potential. The anisotropic turbulent field is strongest at the positions of the prominent dust lanes on the inner edge of the inner gas spiral arms, due to compression of turbulent fields in the density-wave shock. A regular field (composed of modes m = 0 and m = 2 ) also exists in the disk of M 51, but is much weaker than the anisotropic turbulent field fletcher11 . Notably, RMs between 18 cm and 22 cm wavelengths, tracing only the polarized emission from regions nearest to the observer, show a large-scale pattern that indicates a regular field in the halo of M 51 heald+09,fletcher11,mao15 .
The spiral pattern in the gas of M 51 is also distorted and short-lived, so that only a weak large-scale regular field and a rudimentary magnetic arm could develop.
